notes: The organization of quorums of Seventy in the Church was regarded as a very strange thing in modern times, but that such an organization had existed in the Church of God, both in the days of Moses and also in the days of Messiah, is evident from the scriptures. The Lord said to Moses: "Come up unto the Lord, thou, and Aaron, Nadab, and Abihu, and seventy of the elders of Israel; and worship ye afar off. * * * Then went up Moses, and Aaron, Nadab, and Abihu, and seventy of the elders of Israel. * * * And upon the nobles of the children of Israel He laid not His hand: also they saw God, and did eat and drink" (Exodus 24:1, 9, 11). And again, "And the Lord said unto Moses, Gather unto me seventy men of the elders of Israel, whom thou knowest to be the elders of the people, and officers over them; and bring them unto the tabernacle of the congregation, that they may stand there with thee. And I will come down and talk with thee there: and I will take of the spirit which is upon thee, and will put it upon them; and they shall bear the burden of the people with thee, that thou bear it not thyself alone. * * * And Moses went out, and told the people the words of the Lord, and gathered the seventy men of the elders of the people, and set them round about the tabernacle. And the Lord came down in a cloud, and spake

quotes.text: THE order of the Seventy is doubtless of ancient origin, as we may naturally conclude from the writings of Moses, Ex. 24:1, 9, 11, and Numbers 11:16, 17, 24, 25. These references to the Seventy, however, although very significant, are nevertheless so meager that we find difficulty in determining the nature of their duties or in connecting them with the subsequent organizations of that body, either in the meridian of time or in this latter-day dispensation. This much, however, is certain, that their calling was of high spiritual importance in the work of God during the administration of Moses, for we read in paragraph 25 above referred to, that "the Lord came down in a cloud * * * and took of the spirit that was upon him [Moses] and gave it unto the Seventy Elders" and "When it rested upon them they prophesied and did not cease.
quotes.speaker: Rulon S. Wells
quotes.attr: Rulon S. Wells, Improvement Era 1930

quotes.text: It appears probable, from Exodus 24:1, 9, where seventy of the elders of Israel were summoned, as the distinguished men of the people, to see God and to eat the covenant meal before him on the mountain of God, and from Numbers 11:16 24-25), where seventy elders share something of the spirit of Moses, that there was an institution, sanctified by ancient tradition, of seventy elders as the representatives of Israel. What Ezekiel sees therefore [in 8:11] is not a chance group, but the representatives of Israel, as they had once stood before Yahweh at the making of the covenant.
quotes.source: By Study and Also by Faith: Essays in Honor of Hugh W. Nibley on the Occasion of His Eightieth Birthday
quotes.attr: S. Kent Brown in John M. Lundquist and Stephen D. Ricks, eds., By Study and Also by Faith: Essays in Honor of Hugh W. Nibley on the Occasion of His Eightieth Birthday, 27 March 1990, 2 vols., 1:, p. 39

quotes.text: Nadab and Abihu Their introduction without any descriptive identity presupposes knowledge of Aaron's genealogy given in 6: 23. The two later perished while performing some sinful ritual act., Their inclusion in the present delegation and the exclusion of their two younger brothers, who succeeded them in the priesthood,' attest to the antiquity of this tradition.
quotes.speaker: Nahum Sarna
quotes.attr: Nahum Sarna, The JPS Torah Commentary: Exodus, Philadelphia, 1989, 150

quotes.text: The unique relationship between Christ and his disciples in divine government is found throughout religious history. A glimpse into the past reveals the precedence, patterns, and practice of common consent as it underscores its vitality and importance. This principle was practiced in one form or another during the lifetime of Moses (Exodus 24:3), Joshua (Numbers 27:19-22), Peter (Acts 1:26), and Mosiah (Mosiah 29:25-26). According to Elder Bruce R. Mc Conkie, the law of common consent "has been operative in every dispensation." Thus, this principle is of necessity part of modern Church government. Common consent is another of the many witnesses that The Church of Jesus Christ has been literally restored.
quotes.attr: Matthew O. Richardson in Leon R. Hartshorn, Dennis A. Wright, and Craig J. Ostler, eds., The Doctrine and Covenants, a Book of Answers: The 25th Annual Sidney B. Sperry Symposium, p.78

notes: Moses wrote all the words of the Lord— After the people had promised obedience, (Exodus 24:3) and so entered into the bonds of the covenant, “it was necessary,” says Calmet, “to draw up an act by which the memory of these transactions might be preserved, and confirm the covenant by authentic and solemn ceremonies.” And this Moses does. 1. As legislator, he reduces to writing all the articles and conditions of the agreement, with the people’s act of consent. 2. As their mediator and the deputy of the Lord, he accepts on his part the resolution of the people; and Jehovah on his part engages himself to Israel, to be their God, their King, and Protector, and to fulfill to them all the promises he had made to their fathers.

quotes.text: Peace-offerings— Bullocks or goats; see Hebrews 9:19. The blood of these was poured out before the Lord, and then the priests and people might feast on the flesh.
quotes.speaker: Adam Clarke
quotes.attr: Adam Clarke, Bible Commentary Volume 1

quotes.text: Burnt-offerings— They generally consisted of sheep and goats, Leviticus 1:10. These were wholly consumed by fire.
quotes.speaker: Adam Clarke
quotes.attr: Adam Clarke, Bible Commentary - Volume 1

quotes.text: Half of the blood being sprinkled on the Altar, and half of it sprinkled on the People, showed that both God and They were mutually bound by this covenant. God was bound to the People to support, defend, and save them; the People were bound to God to fear, love, and serve him.
quotes.speaker: Adam Clarke
quotes.attr: Adam Clarke, Bible Commentary - Volume 1

quotes.text: Reference is made in both the Old and New Testaments to books and epistles which are not now available. These include: Book of the Covenant (Ex. 24:4, 7); Book of the Wars of the Lord (Num. 21:14); Book of Jasher (Josh. 10:13; 2 Sam. 1:18); A Book of Statutes (1 Sam. 10:25); Book of the Acts of Solomon (1 Kings 11:41); Books of Nathan and Gad (1 Chron. 29:29; 2 Chron. 9:29); Prophecy of Ahijah and Visions of Iddo (2 Chron. 9:29; 12:15; 13:22); Book of Shemaiah (2 Chron. 12:15); Book of Jehu (2 Chron. 20:34); Acts of Uzziah, written by Isaiah (2 Chron. 26:22); Sayings of the Seers (2 Chron. 33:19); an epistle of Paul to the Corinthians 1 Cor. 5:9); an epistle of Paul to the Ephesians (Eph. 3:3); an epistle of Paul to the Laodiceans (Col. 4:16); Epistle of Jude (Jude 3); and the Prophecies of Enoch (Jude 14).
quotes.speaker: Bruce R. Mc Conkie
quotes.attr: Bruce R. Mc Conkie, Mormon Doctrine, 2d ed., p.454

notes: The cedar represented pride and haughtiness, whereas the hyssop symbolized modesty, humility, and purity. Leviticus 14 details its use in the cleansing process for a leper. A hyssop branch was also used in applying blood to the doorposts of Israelite houses on the night that the angel of death passed over (see Ex. 12:21-23); later, Moses used hyssop in sprinkling the blood of the testament on the scriptures and on

quotes.text: In his translation Joseph Smith stresses Christ as the offering for remission of sins. Hebrews' intense imagery features the prefiguring Old Testament sacrifices. There is the solemn Day of Atonement, when through offerings Israel became "clean from all . . . sins before the Lord" (Lev. 16:30). Paul stated the major principle of sacrificial forgiveness: there is "no remission" without "shedding of blood" (Heb. 9:22). When the "new covenant" would come, Jeremiah prophesied, God would "forgive their iniquity" and would "remember their sin no more" (Jer. 31:34). Paul quoted that promise (Heb. 10:17), explaining that Christ made this possible. God's people were first established through the sprinkling of "the blood of the covenant," symbolic of their obedience to God's laws and rites (Ex. 24:8). Paul quoted those historic words of Moses (Heb. 9:20). Jesus had also mirrored them for the meaning of his sacrifice: "For this is my blood of the new covenant [diatheke], which is shed for many for the remission of sins" (Matt. 26:28, literal trans.).
quotes.speaker: Richard Lloyd Anderson
quotes.attr: Richard Lloyd Anderson, Understanding Paul, p.217

quotes.text: Gather unto me seventy men of the elders of Israel, whom thou knowest to be the elders of the people, and officers over them," the Lord commanded Moses, and "they shall bear the burden of the people with thee, that thou bear it not thyself alone." (Num. 11:16-17.) Thus was the Lord's system of government perfected in ancient Israel. Moses served as the prophet, seer, and revelator; at his side were "the princes of Israel," twelve in number, comparable to the Twelve Apostles, one of whom presided over each of the tribes (Num. 7); then came the Seventy-holy and noble men who also sat in judgment and regulated the affairs of the people. And a millennium and a half later, the Jews still maintained the form of their ancient order; the Great Sanhedrin-either patterned after or descended from the Quorum of Seventy called by Moses-still attempted to rule over the people. Albeit, to their sorrow, those of whom it was composed in time's meridian were no longer the spiritual giants who were qualified to go up into the Holy Mount and see "the God of Israel," as had been their predecessors in office. (Ex. 24:9-11.)
quotes.speaker: Bruce R. Mc Conkie
quotes.source: The Mortal Messiah: From Bethlehem to Calvary
quotes.attr: Bruce R. Mc Conkie, The Mortal Messiah: From Bethlehem to Calvary, 4 vols., 4:, p.164

notes: the many references to God's body parts, such as "I [Jacob] have seen God face to face" (Gen. 32:30) "they saw the God of Israel: and there was under his feet" (Ex. 24:10) "the Lord spake unto Moses face to face" (Ex. 33:11) and "I will take away mine hand, and thou shalt see my back parts: but my face shall not be seen" (Ex. 33:23). God also appears embodied in New Testament accounts of divine appearances. For instance, Acts 7:56 tells of Stephen seeing God and the Son of Man standing on the right hand of God. It is hard to imagine a being with a face, feet, hands, and back parts but without a body. (The Doctrine of Divine Embodiment: Restoration, Judeo-Christian, and Philosophical Perspectives, BYU Studies, vol. 35 (1995), Number 3-1996)
More to the point, many biblical passages straightforwardly describe God as embodied. For instance, Gen. 1:26 records that God made man "in our own image, after our likeness." Even more explicit are

quotes.text: One of the first doctrines to disappear when apostasy conceives in the hearts of mortals is the fact that God is a glorified man. When people repress that doctrine, they forget who they really are and what they may become (cf. the Zoramites, Alma 31:15). One of the first things Joseph Smith learned as the Lord undertook to educate him was that he was created in the literal image of the Father and the Son. Many express outrage at this doctrine, believing that it diminishes God and at the same time presumptuously exalts man. But Joseph Smith did not invent the idea. The Bible plainly speaks of man's creation in the image of God (Gen. 1:26-27); Jacob saw God face to face (Gen. 32:30); God has feet (Ex. 24:10); he has a finger (Ex. 31:18); Moses saw his face (Ex. 33:11) and his back parts (Ex. 33:23) and spoke to God mouth to mouth (Num. 12:8). The "eyes of the Lord" and the "ears of the Lord" occur frequently in the Prophets and in Psalms. In Genesis God walks about in the garden. (Genesis 3:8.)
quotes.speaker: Catherine Thomas in Kent P. Jackson
quotes.attr: Catherine Thomas in Kent P. Jackson, ed., Studies in Scripture, Vol. 8: Alma 30 to Moroni, p.173

quotes.text: These verses contain subtle intimations of the two themes that occupy the last section of Exodus (chaps. 25-40). They prepare us for, and make the transition to, the account of the building of the Tabernacle and the episode of the golden calf. Mention of the "stone tablets" (v. 12) is indispensable both for explaining the purpose of constructing the Ark (25:10-22) and for understanding Moses' reaction to the apostasy (32:15--16,19)It was Moses' prolonged stay on the mountain (v. 18) that precipitated the crisis (32:1). The stem sh-k-n used of God's Presence on Sinai (v. to) is also employed in connection with the Tabernacle (25:8,19; 40:34-38). The Divine Presence (kevod YHVH) rests on Sinai (vv. 15,17) and in the completed Tabernacle (40:34-35), and the cloud covers the mountain (v. 15) and the Tent of Meeting (40:34). Finally, Joshua's partial ascent of Sinai (vv. 13-14) explains his puzzled reaction to the worshipers' noisy revelry around the calf (32:17-18).
quotes.speaker: Nahum Sarna
quotes.attr: Nahum Sarna, The JPS Torah Commentary: Exodus, Philadelphia, 1989, 153

quotes.text: Come up to Me Either Moses had descended with the entire delegation and is now instructed to ascend once again or he is still on the mountain and is directed to ascend to its highest level. The former is favored by verses 13-14, which imply that he has been down among the people. The latter is supported by the parallel account in Deuteronomy 5:28 in which Moses is told, "But you remain here with Me." Two strands of tradition appear to have been interwoven here.
quotes.speaker: Nahum Sarna
quotes.attr: Nahum Sarna, The JPS Torah Commentary: Exodus, Philadelphia, 1989, 153

notes: The President of the High Priesthood and his two counselors constitute the First Presidency of the Church. Moses was the president of the Church in his day, and he was aided by his brother Aaron (who, as we shall soon see, had another important position in the Israelite church or community); Joshua, his

quotes.text: The Presence of the Lord. Lehi feared that Laman and Lemuel might be cast off from the Presence of the Lord. This Presence is what is order Jewish writings is called the Shekinah, referring to the indwelling of the Lord in his Sanctuary. It was the Shekinah, or indwelling, also called "the Glory of the Lord" that rested on Mt. Sinai, for the Lord "called Moses out of the midst of a cloud, and the sight of the Glory of the Lord was like devouring fire on the top of the mountain in the eyes of the children of Israel." (Ex. 24:15-17)It was a manifestation of Light in the pillar of cloud that guided Israel through the wilderness. (Ex. 40:34-38) "My Presence shall go with thee, and I will give thee rest." (Ex. 33:14) It was the Shekinah that filled the Temple of Solomon. (2 Chron. 7:1-3) The Prophet Ezekiel in his vision, sees the Shekinah departing from the temple. (Ez. 10:19; 11:22) and then again entering into the new temple. (Ez. 43:2-5) We can understand from this that to be cast out from "the Presence of the Lord" is to become spiritually lost.
quotes.speaker: George Reynolds and Janne M. Sjodahl
quotes.source: Commentary on the Book of Mormon
quotes.attr: George Reynolds and Janne M. Sjodahl, Commentary on the Book of Mormon, edited and arranged by Philip C. Reynolds, 7 vols., 1:, p.64-65

quotes.text: Clouds represent the veil through which we must pass to stand in the divine presence (Ex. 19:9, 16; 24:15; Luke 9:34-35).
quotes.attr: Joseph Fielding Mc Conkie and Donald W. Parry, A Guide to Scriptural Symbols, p.32

quotes.text: To ancient Israel one of the important aspects of God was his ability to display his power through the manifestation of burning light. Indeed, Israel stood in awe because of the display of a brilliance like a devouring inferno on the top of Sinai. (Exodus 24:17.) Moses proclaimed, "the Lord thy God is a consuming fire." (Deuteronomy 4:24.) His presence was manifest on more than one occasion by a pillar of fire, which gave light to Israel but vexed the Egyptians. (Exodus 13:21; 14:24.) The cloud of his glory dwelt upon the tabernacle while its radiance filled the court. (Exodus 40:34.) That this idea has continued into the present can be seen in the promise to the early Saints that "this generation shall not all pass away until an house shall be built unto the Lord, and a cloud shall rest upon it, which cloud shall be even the glory of the Lord, which shall fill the house." (D&C 84:5.)
quotes.source: Doctrines for Exaltation: The 1989 Sperry Symposium on the Doctrine and Covenants
quotes.attr: Richard D. Draper in Susan Easton Black et al., Doctrines for Exaltation: The 1989 Sperry Symposium on the Doctrine and Covenants, p.32

notes: manner of workmanship. For centuries thereafter this portable tabernacle, constructed about 1200 B.C. served the people of Israel as their temple and was known as the Tent or Dwelling, The Sanctuary or the Tabernacle. The Tent of Jehovah. It was replaced by a permanent structure built by Solomon about 975 B.C. The Lord specified all the materials, design, clothing, to be used; placement of objects. This is all recorded in Exodus. We can assume that because God is perfect, that all specifications have significance, and that we can profit from a study of them. The path taken symbolically by an Israelite from outside, into the Holy of Holies, was typical or symbolic of man's journey from this world into exaltation. Wood and brass were materials common to the Outer Court, but the glory of Gold was the standard for the precincts of the inner tabernacle. There very placement of the tabernacle in the midst of Israel was symbolically signifi-cant, for the tabernacle symbolized the living presence of Jehovah, thus Israel's life and existence was to center in the Lord. The camp was laid out and organized using God as their central orientation. In actuality, the tabernacle complex consisted of two main divisions; an outer courtyard, and the Tabernacle proper. The courtyard measured 100 cubits, or about 150 feet east to west, and 50 cubits, or about 75 feet north to south. The Tabernacle itself consisted of two chambers or rooms, divided by a veil. The first, which stood 2/3 of the total floor space was called the Holy Place. Only certain officiating priests could enter this place to perform the daily ordinances. The inner chamber, the final 1/3, was called the "Most Holy Place" or the "Holy of Holies." It was a perfect cubit of 10 cubits, height, width and breadth, and could only be entered once a year on the Day of Atonement. Whereas the whole tabernacle complex was viewed as the House of the Lord, the Holy of Holies was seen as the representation of the place where God actually dwelt. Thus the Holy of Holies symbolized the Celestial Kingdom, just as the Celestial rooms in modern temples do. The entrance to the outer court, as well as the Tabernacle itself, faced eastward; It is of course the direction from which light originates in the morning. Oriented toward the light. Inside the courtyard sat the Altar of Sacrifice, made of shittem wood, overlaid with brass. This was the altar on which all the offerings were made. Here the offerer met the Priest, who represented the Lord, in consummating his covenants and was symbolically cleansed. The altar stood 3 cubits high, about 4.5 feet, and was 5 cubits, 7.5 feet square. Four rings were attached at the corners of the altar, through which staves were passed. These staves enabled the altar to be transported. On each corner of the altar a horn was fashioned, as one scholar indicates, these horns were projections on each corner to represent the horns of oxen, and in these the whole force of the altar was concentrated. Horn is commonly used in the scriptures as a representation of power. Horns on an animal are the source of his might and power. One of the root meaning of "horn" in Hebrew is "power." Christ is called the HORN OF SALVATION because of his power to save. Thus the horns of the altar may have signified the power of the Priesthood to administer the ordinances and have the acceptable to Jehovah. Often the blood of the sacrifice on the altar was smeared on the horns, suggesting that power of the atoning blood was being evoked. In this way the children of Israel were reminded of the power of the Lord to save them. The basin was placed between the altar and the doorway of the tabernacle, and was filled with water. It was used for filling the hands and feet of the priests before they officiated at the altar of burnt offerings, and also prior to their entering into the Tabernacle. It represented their need to be clean and presentable before officiating in the sacred ordinances of the tabernacle. The twelve oxen which later supported the laver in the temple of Solomon, represented the twelve tribes of Israel; the water symbolized the baptism required for entry into the House of Israel. Inside the tabernacle were the two rooms. They were separated by a veil. Respectively they symbolize the Terrestrial and the Celestial Kingdoms, while the outer courtyard represented the Telestial Kingdom. The Tent area represented the house or Dwelling place of God. " BN: What was done with each vessel in the tabernacle? They anointed it, which made it consecrated and holy. They did that also the priests. Think about it. The Altar represents Christ, The laver, waters of Light, Christ. The table of shewbread the lamp stand, Christ. Prayers of Israel at the veil, going into

Priesthood; a set of ceremonial rites as additions. All this was to keep Israel in a remembrance of God from day to day, blood and burnt offerings. Recall that Adam and Abraham has "first-fruits" to offer, and cereal offerings, and so forth. Even when Abraham sliced up the carcasses, those were the only ones accepted under the law of Moses. A slide presentation was given showing the tabernacle, and the order of the priesthood; the sacrifices that were offered. The Garden of Eden was the first temple (James E. Talmage). Adam and Eve were married there in the presence of God and commanded to multiply and replenish the earth. When the history of human thought is written from the point of view of temple worship, it may be found that temples and the work done in them have been the dominating influence shaping human thought from the beginning of the race. (John A. Widstoe) Early scripture accounts, the sacred heights of holy mounts were the places where God revealed the priesthood and its higher ordinances to the prophets. Moses, Enoch, Brother of Jared, Nephi, Abraham, all received revelation on mountains. It was at Bethel that Jacob saw the vision of the ladder in which Angels ascended and descended. MGR said because he had met the Lord there, Jacob named the site "Beth-el" named after the House of the Lord. The gate of heaven. Jacob not only passed through the gate of heaven, but by living up to every covenant, he went all the way down the path. Marion G. Romney concludes that temples are to us what Beth-el was to Jacob. Even more, they are also the gates to heaven for all our unendowed kindred dead. We should all do our duty in bringing our loved ones to them. From Adam to Moses the earthly sanctuary of the Lord had often been in the mountain heights or other sacred places. In the days of Moses it became different. From the scriptures we read, that the Lord says unto Moses, "Speak unto the children of Israel and let them make me a sanctuary that I may dwell among them." By direct revelation the children of Israel were to receive a temple, a holy sanctuary where God could come and reveal the principles and ordinances of exaltation. A House of the Lord would be built among them so they could have access to sacred revelation. The Tabernacle was provided so that the ordinances might be revealed that had been hidden, including the temple ordinances for the living. President Joseph Fielding Smith explained by such ordinances are so critical: "You cannot receive an exaltation until you have made covenants in the House of the Lord and received keys and authority that are there bestowed, and which cannot be given in any other place on the earth today." Moses taught the children of Israel that the Higher Priesthood held the keys to the mysteries of God, that in the ordinances of the Higher Priesthood, the power of Godliness was manifest, and without those ordinances no man could see the face of God and live. He also sought diligently to sanctify his people so that through those ordinances they might behold the face of God. But Israel sinned and refused to sanctify themselves, and they lost that opportunity. They could not endure his presence because of the hardness of their hearts. Thus the fulness of the temple blessings were lost to ancient Israel's eternal shame. Nevertheless, Moses was directed to establish among them the lesser priesthood, with the keys of the preparatory gospel, including the gospel of repentance, baptism for remission of sins, and the law of carnal commandments. When Moses was called back to Mt. Sinai for the second time, the Lord told him: "It shall not be according to the first, for I will take away the priesthood out of their midst; therefore my holy order, and the ordinances thereof shall not go before them. But I will give unto them the law, as at first, but it shall be after the law of the carnal commandment." Thus Israel of denied the ordinances of the holy Priesthood, nevertheless, the fulness of the temple ordinances was taken away, the Lord did not revoke his plan to bless Israel through his wilderness temple. The Tabernacle, or portable temple sanctuary was still to be built. When Moses came down from the Holy Mount following a second 40 day period with the Lord, the Children of Israel were receptive and anxious to respond to their Prophet leader. Anticipating the construction of the tabernacle, they brought their precious possessions, in such abundance, that Moses found it necessary to restrain them. The Lord chose two men referred to as artificers, and take the materials offered by Israel, and fashion them into a building. They were filled by the spirit of God in wisdom, understanding and knowledge, and in all

The purpose was to bring them to Christ. Christ revealed the law to Israel, the very One who gave it. The higher law was taken away because Israel rejected it. What the High Priest did, under the law of Moses, was what everyone was originally supposed to do. They would all have gone through the entire ritual, each one all the way into the Holy of Holies. The law parred it down. The first tablets that Moses brought down from the mountain was designed to put everyone through the veil. When the law of Moses came, only the High Priest could go through the veil, once a year. The first was designed to take everyone through. So to understand what the law of designed to represent required that you watch the order of the ceremony by the Priesthood. See Alma 13. When you watch them at the altar, who did you have to pretend was really at the altar? Yourself. They represented you at the altar. While they were in the wilderness, the firstborn sons of every family were supposed to do the temple service. Then Jehovah changed it, you redeem them by sacrifice, and the tribe of Levi would represent the firstborn. But remember that everything is symbolic of the Firstborn. They left Egypt as a group called the "firstborn"..."Israel is my son, even my firstborn; therefore if you won't let my son go, I will slay your firstborn." Israel is a system of firstborns, and everyone in Israel, in the covenant becomes firstborn, and the name of the church in the Celestial Kingdom is the "Church of the Firstborn." They inherit all the Father has, keep covenant, become Priests, Kings, Priestesses and Queens, royal and holy. The law of Moses, the preparatory gospel, was a schoolmaster. Paul taught that the law was added to something. Joseph Smith taught that it was added to the gospel. The Ten Commandments, and the law of Sacrifices had started with Adam, and he knew the principles and ordinances of the gospel. The law of Moses introduced the carnal commandments, the preparatory gospel, administered through the Aaronic

quotes.text: The Exodus and The Tabernacle" I'm going to begin this evening by reviewing with you a scripture that we looked at last year about this time. It's a very important scripture. It's Moses 6:63. Behold all things have their likeness, and all things are created and made to bear record of me, both things which are temporal, and things which are spiritual; things which are in the heavens above, things which are on the earth, things which are in the earth, things which are under the earth, both above and beneath: all things bear record of me. I refer to that as the signature of the Lord. There is something very unique about what I'm going to show you tonight. What's unique is that absolutely no man can duplicate it. No man can make it happen. Only the Lord can make it happen. Let me read you a statement by James Plasterus. Listen to what he says. We are going to talk about the Exodus. The God of Exodus is the name of his book. It was the Exodus which shaped all of Israel's understanding of history. It was only in light of the Exodus, that Israel was able to look back into the past to gather her early history. It was also the Exodus which provided the prophets with the key to the understanding of Israel's future. In this sense, the Exodus stands at the center of Israel's history. This is a non-member who wrote that. That's a very true statement. I don't know if Dr. Plasterus really understands the real impact of how the Exodus really is the true history of Israel. We are going to cover that in a moment. But before we do, I must show you something; about the tabernacle that they ended up carrying into the wilderness. 'This is the floor plan of the tabernacle. There is a lot to be said about that, more than we can say in one hour. This is the east end - the west end. For your information, Judah camped at the east and Ephraim camped at the west. If you think of the implications of that: the sun rose on Judah, it set on Ephraim. One was the political leadership, the other was the ecclesiastical leadership of the house of Israel. But think further into the history of the world, what this was really a prototype of, which we'll cover in some detail, if we have time tonight, and that is: Where does Judah build its capital in the world, in the east? And where does Ephraim build his capital in the world, in the west? What's happening with the tabernacle, its appurtenances, something is being foreshadowed? Remember what we just read, Moses, that everything that the Lord does will be a foreshadowing of Him and His great work. He says, ' '''All things bear record of me: If I do something, temporal, or spiritual, whether it's above the earth, on It or in it, It will all be in some kind of a pattern that will show my work." Are you, following me so far, with the east and the west? When you came Into the tabernacle, ,the first thing that you saw was the burning altar, of incense.) Then there was the laver, then there was a kind of veil here and you went into what is referred to as the Holy Place. This was the lamp stand. It has 7 different candles, actually we call them candlesticks.
quotes.speaker: Robert Norman
quotes.attr: Robert Norman, YC Stake Education Symposium 2/84

quotes.text: The Ground Plan The Tabernacle is an oblong structure comprising three zones. These are, in descending order of holiness: the Holy of Holies, the Holy Place, and the Court. The structure is oriented longitudinally, on an east-west axis, with the most sacred zone in the west. An outer: perimeter demarcates the sacred area. This is divided into two equal squares. The first two zones lie in one square and the Court constitutes the other square. From the Ark in the Holy of Holies, God reaches out to Israel; from the altar of sacrifice, the Israelites reach out to God. Each seems to be located exactly at the point of intersection of the diagonals of the squares. See diagram, page 155. Ancient Parallels Mobile sanctuaries are known to us from Arab Bedouin practices, pre-Islamic and Islamic, and even from pre-Christian times. They are also documented in Phoenician and Egyptian sources, the latter from the period of Rameses II (ca. 1290-1224 B.C.E.). Furthermore, the method of construction described here in our chapters is now known to be based on well-established Egyptian techniques.' The Celestial Blueprint A prominent characteristic of the narrative in both its parts is the repeated reference to divinely given instructions and celestial patterns for the terrestrial edifice and for its contents.2 Such a conception of a sanctuary is not unknown elsewhere in the ancient world. It is attested as early as about 2200 B.C.E. in the narration of a building project by the Sumerian King Gudea of Lagash. It also occurs in Egyptian texts that treat of similar enterprises. This idea of divine inspiration, initiation, and specification of a religious institution generally communicates the deity's sanction and acceptance of the sacred structure, which is thereby endowed with legitimacy.3 The Tabernacle and Creation The series of instructions for the components of the Tabernacle is made up of seven subsections, each of which is introduced by the formula "The Lord spoke/said to Moses."4 Six of them deal with creativity, and the seventh features the Sabbath law. This latter is explicitly grounded in creation. The association of the Tabernacle with the Sabbath is given prominence in Leviticus 19:30 and 26:2: "You shall keep my Sabbaths and venerate My sanctuary: I am the Lord." Further, the completed Tabernacle is erected on New Year's day, as Exodus 40:17 records. This underscores the idea that a new era in the life of the people has begun and the cosmogonic association of the Tabernacle is thereby strengthened. Finally, other thematic and verbal echoes of the Genesis Creation narrative in our accounts confirm their interdependence.
quotes.speaker: Nahum Sarna
quotes.attr: Nahum Sarna, The JPS Torah Commentary: Exodus, Philadelphia, 1989, 155-156

quotes.text: Christ (Moses 1) at a larger level. The tabernacle was a small model of the larger plan. Sinai was a larger version, model, of the same plan. Moses typified Christ, the Deliverer, in the Exodus. Sinai was the temple. The Tabernacle represents the Exodus, and Sinai, at a smaller level. Moses presided over the larger; Egypt, Exodus, Promised land are models of telestial, terrestrial and celestial. There is an even larger model; The earth! Christ is the High Priest. He is the one who takes the "real sacrifice", takes us through the veil into the "real millennium" and takes us into the real Celestial Kingdom. Again, there are different levels of the same story, or same plan. Notice, if you reject Joseph Smith, and the restoration, what happens to your perception of the plan, and the model. You lose the key to under-standing the model. Why? Without the ordinances of the Priesthood, you don't have the "key of knowledge" of God, and therefore the "power of godliness is not manifest. Without the ordinances you cannot find out the ways of God. (D&C 84:19-21) This is because the ordinances model the plan. They reveal through the prophet and the spirit those things which help us understand the mysteries of God. If you reject Joseph Smith or the Temple, like the RLDS, you cannot no longer interpret Israel, its history, nor the plan of God. It takes the ordinances to open up the mysteries of Godliness and once that is opened up, you can see the nature of God everywhere, over and over again. When they get to Sinai, it was impressive. The Ten Commandments were given for everyone to hear. Jehovah heard them spoken off the Mount. Notice the low level of spirituality of the Israelites. Moses comes off the Mount, and they tell him they don't want to hear God's voice again. Its OK for Moses to go up and find out what God wants them to do, but they don't want to hear his voice. They though they would die when God spoke. The Old Testament doesn't help us much as to why they thought this, but the Book of Mormon is helpful. 3 Nephi 11:3 3 And it came to pass that while they were thus conversing one with another, they heard a voice as if it had come out of heaven; and they cast their eyes round about, for they understood not the voice which they heard; and it was not a harsh voice, neither was it a loud voice; nevertheless and notwithstanding it being a small voice, it did pierce them to the center, INSOMUCH THAT THERE WAS NO PART OF THEIR FRAME THAT IT DID NOT CAUSE TO QUAKE; YEA, IT DID PIERCE THEM TO THE VERY SOUL, AND DID CAUSE THEIR HEARTS TO BURN.
quotes.speaker: Robert Norman
quotes.attr: Robert Norman, 1989 Old Testament Class Notes

quotes.text: Israel into Heaven...to Christ. The veil..Christ. Mercy at the judgment seat...from Christ. The tabernacle was a model of the plan of Salvation executed by Christ. Therefore, Christ must be superimposed on the tabernacle itself. True worship of Christ then is made and developed in the Rock, the Stone...of the temple. If you are going to found your life, there is only one place you can do it. That is in the temple...where the stone is. The same way Jacob did it. At the place known as the gateway of heaven. The temple and Christ are designed to be one and the same thing. Recall also, knowing that Christ was about to be crucified, he cleansed the "OUTER COURT" of the temple the last week of his life, knowing they were going to kill the "cornerstone" or him. After he cleanses it...that is, wipes out the wicked from the Telestial world, he walks out and says that one stone won't be left standing on another. When you pull the cornerstone out, it will topple. He is the cornerstone, and the person upon whom the ordinances thereof are established and centered. The simple thing like the stone in Genesis 28 with Jacob set the pattern for temples as a representation of Christ in all the scriptures. Therefore, when you go into the temple, as Peter taught his people, you become a "lively stone." Until you go in and enter into the covenants, you cannot yourself become a lively stone. Recall that his name was also changed to the Stone or Rock. I Peter 2:3-9
quotes.speaker: Robert Norman
quotes.attr: Robert Norman, 1989 Old Testament Class Notes
